threats.title: Zero-Percentage Claim Exploit Allows Free Sweeping of Contract ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: The `claim` function accepts a `_percentage` parameter that is not validated to be greater than 0. If `_percentage = 0` (or if `userStake * _percentage < 100` causing rounding to 0), `userClaim` becomes 0. The 0x swap executes successfully for 0 tokens, and the user's balance remains unchanged. However, the function then mints `lpETH` based on the ENTIRE `address(this).balance`. An attacker can repeatedly call this to drain any ETH that accumulates in the contract without spending their LRT tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: This combines a missing range check with an absolute balance capture, creating a repeatable, zero-cost exploit that drains protocol funds.
preconditions: Attacker has a non-zero balance of any allowed LRT token
Contract holds some ETH balance (e.g., from receive(), selfdestruct, or residual swap dust)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing lower bound check on percentage combined with absolute balance usage
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: userClaim can be 0, leaving balances[msg.sender][_token] unchanged, while claimedAmount captures all contract ETH

analysis_questions: Does the 0x exchange proxy revert on 0-amount swaps, or does it succeed?
Can an attacker force ETH into the contract to exploit this?
Does the balance reduction `userStake - 0` correctly leave the user's balance intact for repeated calls?
validation_requirements: Write a test calling claim with _percentage = 0 when contract has ETH balance
Verify the attacker receives lpETH
Verify the attacker's LRT balance is not reduced

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Contract accumulates 5 ETH via accidental direct transfers. 2) Attacker deposits 1 wei of an LRT. 3) Attacker calls claim(LRT, 0, ...). 4) Swap executes for 0 amount. 5) Attacker receives 5 lpETH. 6) Attacker's LRT balance remains 1 wei.
prerequisites: Any ETH present in the contract post-conversion

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drain of any ETH sent to the contract post-conversion
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users who accidentally send ETH, or protocol fee accumulation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, lpETH is minted directly to the attacker

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for edge-case _percentage values (0) and residual ETH balances

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily visible on-chain via Claimed events with 0 token reduction but high claimedAmount

threats.title: Hardcoded Calldata Offsets Allow UniswapV3 Swap Path Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The `_decodeUniswapV3Data` function uses hardcoded assembly offsets (e.g., `add(p, 128)`) to extract the `inputToken` and `outputToken` from the 0x UniswapV3 swap calldata. This assumes standard ABI encoding where the `bytes` payload offset is exactly 0x80 (128). An attacker can craft non-standard ABI encoding, placing a 'fake' path at the hardcoded offset to pass validation, while placing the 'real' path at a different offset which the 0x proxy will actually execute.
threats.investigation_rationale: ABI decoding via hardcoded offsets is a known anti-pattern that allows offset spoofing, enabling attackers to bypass validation checks while executing malicious payloads.
preconditions: Attacker is claiming an LRT token via UniswapV3 exchange route

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Hardcoded calldata offsets for dynamic bytes arrays
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The validation reads from a fixed memory location, but the actual 0x proxy uses standard ABI decoding which follows the dynamic offset pointer
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Proper ABI decoding (abi.decode) or dynamic offset resolution in assembly

analysis_questions: Can an attacker route the swap through a malicious pool with extreme slippage?
Can the attacker extract the value difference to their own address during the swap?
validation_requirements: Construct a calldata payload with a non-standard bytes offset
Place valid tokens at the hardcoded offset and malicious tokens at the real offset
Verify that `_validateData` passes but 0x proxy executes the malicious path

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Attacker calls claim with crafted calldata. 2) offset pointer is set to 0x100. 3) At 0x80, attacker places fake path (LRT -> WETH). 4) At 0x100, attacker places real path (LRT -> AttackerToken -> WETH). 5) Validation passes. 6) 0x proxy executes real path, allowing attacker to extract value via their custom token pool.
prerequisites: Attacker must deploy a malicious Uniswap V3 pool to route the swap through

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Attacker can steal the ETH value of their own LRT tokens during the swap, effectively double-dipping (getting value from swap + getting lpETH)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Protocol liquidity (attacker extracts value that should have gone to lpETH)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing fuzzing or negative tests with non-standard ABI encodings

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Requires deep calldata inspection to detect offset spoofing

threats.title: Unvalidated TransformERC20 Transformations Enable Arbitrary Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: When using the `TransformERC20` exchange option, `_validateData` only verifies the `selector`, `inputToken`, `outputToken`, and `inputTokenAmount`. It completely ignores the `transformations` array in the 0x calldata. The 0x `transformERC20` function executes these transformations, which can include arbitrary contract calls. If the contract has any residual token approvals to the 0x proxy, an attacker can craft a transformation to steal those tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Forwarding unvalidated complex payloads to a proxy that executes arbitrary logic is highly dangerous, especially when the proxy holds approvals from the calling contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only validates selector, inputToken, outputToken, and amount. Ignores transformations array.

preconditions: Contract has residual token approvals to the 0x exchangeProxy (e.g., from previous partial fills)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The transformations array is not parsed or restricted, allowing arbitrary execution within the 0x proxy context
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Validation of the transformations array or strict whitelisting of allowed transformation types

analysis_questions: What specific transformations are available in the deployed 0x proxy?
Can a transformation execute a `transferFrom` using the PrelaunchPoints contract's residual allowance?
validation_requirements: Identify a 0x transformation that allows arbitrary calls or token transfers
Craft a payload using that transformation to transfer tokens from PrelaunchPoints to the attacker
Execute the payload via `claim`

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) A previous legitimate claim leaves a residual allowance of TokenA to the 0x proxy. 2) Attacker calls claim with a tiny amount of TokenB using TransformERC20. 3) The calldata includes a transformation instructing the 0x proxy to call `TokenA.transferFrom(PrelaunchPoints, attacker, residualAmount)`. 4) Validation passes because TokenB details are correct. 5) 0x proxy executes the transformation, stealing TokenA.
prerequisites: Residual token allowances to the 0x proxy

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of any tokens with residual allowances to the 0x proxy
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users whose tokens are stolen from the contract balance

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for malicious TransformERC20 transformations

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via unexpected Transfer events originating from the 0x proxy

threats.title: Emergency Mode Fails to Unblock ETH Depositors Post-Conversion
threats.hypothesis_description: The `withdraw` function is designed to allow users to retrieve their funds. `emergencyMode` is intended to bypass time restrictions. However, for ETH withdrawals, the check `if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead();` is executed unconditionally, OUTSIDE the `!emergencyMode` block. If the `lpETH` contract is broken, paused, or malicious after conversion, ETH depositors are permanently locked out because `withdraw` reverts and `claim` fails.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a severe logic flaw in the emergency escape hatch, completely defeating its purpose for the primary asset (ETH) once the protocol transitions to the claim phase.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) { revert UseClaimInstead(); } executes regardless of emergencyMode

preconditions: Owner has called convertAllETH (startClaimDate is set)
lpETH contract is non-functional, paused, or malicious, preventing claim()
Owner activates emergencyMode to rescue users

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: State-based revert inside an emergency rescue function
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The UseClaimInstead revert is not bypassed by emergencyMode, permanently locking ETH depositors if claiming is impossible
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Condition should be: if (!emergencyMode && block.timestamp >= startClaimDate)

analysis_questions: Is there any other way for ETH depositors to recover funds if lpETH is broken?
Can the owner use recoverERC20 to rescue the lpETH?
validation_requirements: Set up a mock lpETH that reverts on transfer
Call convertAllETH
Enable emergency mode
Attempt to withdraw ETH -> verify it reverts with UseClaimInstead

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) lpETH contract is upgraded and becomes broken. 2) Owner enables emergencyMode. 3) LRT depositors successfully withdraw their LRTs. 4) ETH depositors attempt to withdraw ETH. 5) Transaction reverts with UseClaimInstead. 6) ETH depositors attempt to claim. 7) Transaction reverts because lpETH transfer fails. 8) Funds are permanently locked.
prerequisites: Failure of the external lpETH dependency

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lockup of all ETH deposits
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, as recoverERC20 explicitly blocks recovering lpETH

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests for emergency mode post-conversion

threats.title: Absolute Balance Usage in LRT Claim Sweeps Unrelated ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: Even without the 0-percentage exploit, the `_claim` function for LRTs uses `claimedAmount = address(this).balance` to determine how much ETH to deposit into `lpETH`. It does not calculate the delta (`postBalance - preBalance`) from the 0x swap. Any legitimate LRT claimer will inadvertently (or intentionally) sweep any unrelated ETH sitting in the contract into their own `lpETH` position.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using absolute contract balance instead of operation delta is a classic vulnerability pattern that leads to sweeping of unrelated funds, breaking the isolation between different users' operations.
preconditions: Contract holds unrelated ETH (e.g., from receive() or selfdestruct)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Absolute balance capture instead of delta
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Fails to isolate the ETH generated by the specific user's swap from the rest of the contract's ETH balance

analysis_questions: How likely is it for the contract to hold unrelated ETH post-conversion?
Can an attacker front-run a legitimate claim with a direct ETH transfer to grief them? (N/A due to no mempool, but still relevant for state)
validation_requirements: Send 1 ETH to the contract via receive()
Perform a legitimate LRT claim that swaps for 0.5 ETH
Verify the user receives 1.5 lpETH instead of 0.5 lpETH

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) A user accidentally sends 2 ETH to the contract. 2) An attacker notices and calls claim() with a valid LRT swap. 3) The swap yields 0.1 ETH. 4) The contract deposits 2.1 ETH to lpETH on behalf of the attacker. 5) The attacker steals the 2 ETH.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of accidentally sent ETH or protocol dust

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for claims when contract has pre-existing ETH balance

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible via Claimed events with disproportionately high claimedAmount

threats.title: Residual Token Approvals to 0x Exchange Proxy
threats.hypothesis_description: In `_fillQuote`, the contract approves the 0x `exchangeProxy` to spend `_amount` of `_sellToken`. If the 0x swap does not consume the entire allowance (e.g., due to partial fills, specific swap routing, or malicious calldata that intentionally leaves allowance), the approval is never reset to 0. This leaves a standing allowance for the 0x proxy to spend the contract's tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: Failing to reset approvals to zero after an external call is a known risk, especially when interacting with complex proxies that can be manipulated via arbitrary calldata (like TransformERC20).

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)); ... no subsequent approval reset to 0

preconditions: A swap is executed that does not consume the full approved amount

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: The contract trusts the 0x proxy to only take what it needs, but leaves the door open if the proxy is compromised or manipulated

analysis_questions: Can a user craft 0x calldata that intentionally consumes less than `_amount`?
Can this residual allowance be exploited via TransformERC20?
validation_requirements: Execute a swap that leaves residual allowance
Verify the allowance persists after the transaction
Attempt to use the residual allowance in a subsequent transaction

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Attacker calls claim with calldata that swaps only 1 wei of the token, leaving the rest of the approved amount as residual allowance. 2) Attacker uses TransformERC20 in a subsequent call to drain the residual allowance.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential theft of tokens if combined with TransformERC20 exploit

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying allowance is 0 after _fillQuote

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Check on-chain allowances of PrelaunchPoints to exchangeProxy

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Break Contract Solvency Accounting
threats.hypothesis_description: The `lock` function uses `safeTransferFrom` to pull `_amount` of tokens and immediately credits the user's balance with `_amount`. If the owner whitelists a fee-on-transfer token, the contract will receive less than `_amount`, but the user's balance will reflect the full `_amount`. This breaks the solvency invariant, meaning the contract will not hold enough tokens to satisfy all user withdrawals or claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the documentation states 'ONLY add wrapped LRT tokens', there is no on-chain enforcement. If a whitelisted token implements a fee (or is upgraded to include one), the core accounting is permanently corrupted.
deployment_assumptions: Owner whitelists a token that has or upgrades to a fee-on-transfer mechanism
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.s.sol uses an `allowedTokens` array passed to the constructor. The specific tokens are not defined in the script, relying on deployment configuration.
preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is whitelisted via allowToken or constructor

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Crediting requested amount instead of actual received amount
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: balances mapping will exceed actual contract holdings
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: uint256 balanceBefore = token.balanceOf(this); transfer...; uint256 actualAmount = token.balanceOf(this) - balanceBefore;

analysis_questions: Are any of the intended allowed tokens (weETH, ezETH, etc.) capable of taking fees or rebasing?
Can the owner accidentally whitelist a problematic token?
validation_requirements: Deploy with a mock fee-on-transfer token
Lock tokens
Attempt to withdraw all tokens -> verify the last withdrawer reverts due to insufficient balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Owner whitelists a token that takes a 1% fee. 2) User A deposits 100 tokens. Contract receives 99. User A balance is 100. 3) User B deposits 100 tokens. Contract receives 99. User B balance is 100. Total contract balance is 198. 4) User A withdraws 100 tokens. Contract balance is 98. 5) User B attempts to withdraw 100 tokens -> reverts. User B loses funds.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of funds for late withdrawers/claimers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Late withdrawers/claimers of the specific token

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for non-standard ERC20 behaviors (fee-on-transfer)
